data.text: a state of intense or overwhelming fear
examples: overcome by terror
people fleeing the scene in terror
He lived in terror of being caught.
a regime that rules by terror
an act of terror
the war on terror
the terrors of war
I stood before fierce Mrs. Mabel Johnston, his secretary and the terror of the office.
Before rifles were available to Eskimo hunters, bears were truly the terror of their existence.
The kid is a little terror.

data.text: violence or the threat of violence used as a weapon of intimidation or coercion; especially: violent or destructive acts (such as bombing) committed by groups in order to intimidate a population or government into granting their demands —sometimes used before another noun
examples: overcome by terror
people fleeing the scene in terror
He lived in terror of being caught.
a regime that rules by terror
an act of terror
the war on terror
the terrors of war
I stood before fierce Mrs. Mabel Johnston, his secretary and the terror of the office.
Before rifles were available to Eskimo hunters, bears were truly the terror of their existence.
The kid is a little terror.

data.text: a very frightening or terrifying aspect
examples: overcome by terror
people fleeing the scene in terror
He lived in terror of being caught.
a regime that rules by terror
an act of terror
the war on terror
the terrors of war
I stood before fierce Mrs. Mabel Johnston, his secretary and the terror of the office.
Before rifles were available to Eskimo hunters, bears were truly the terror of their existence.
The kid is a little terror.

data.text: a sudden awakening in dazed terror that occurs in children during slow-wave sleep, is often preceded by a sudden shrill cry uttered in sleep, and is not remembered when the child awakes —usually used in plural
a state or a period of time marked by violence often committed by those in power that produces widespread terror
of, relating to, or being measures taken to combat or prevent terrorism: counterterrorist
examples: counter-terror measures/operations
a counter-terror expert

enrichment.etymology: Middle English, from Anglo-French terrour, from Latin terror, from terrēre to frighten; akin to Greek trein to be afraid, flee, tremein to tremble
